1: kashseleg baqqayitz — “like snow in summer.” The two meteorological impossibilities — snow at harvest, rain at planting-time (more precisely: qatzir is the dry harvest season) — frame the social impossibility of kavod le-khesil (honor for a fool). Kesil (H3684, the self-confident fool who is morally insensitive rather than merely ignorant) receiving honor is as incongruous and destructive as unseasonal weather ruining the crops. The comparison encodes the claim that social recognition systems have a moral ecology that is disrupted by misplaced honor.
2: ke-tzippor la-nud kadsror la-'uf — “like a sparrow in its flitting, like a swallow in its flying.” The birds in aimless motion go nowhere in particular; applied to qillelat chinnam lo' tavo' — “a causeless curse does not land.” Chinnam (H2600, without cause/ground, for nothing) qualifies the curse: a curse without genuine ground to land on cannot alight, just as a swallow not aimed at anything particular just circles. The aphorism reflects the wisdom tradition’s belief that curses are not mere speech-acts but instruments that require a moral landing-spot to be effective.
3: shot la-sus umetheg la-chamor — “a whip for the horse, a bridle for the donkey.” The tools of animal control (shot, whip; mtheg, bridle) are paired with the appropriate instrument for human correction: shevet le-gev kesilim — “a rod for the back of fools.” The rod (shevet, also a tribal scepter and a shepherd’s staff) applied to the fool’s back is the appropriate instrument for those who have not responded to verbal instruction. The tool-to-subject matching implies that each category of being has its appropriate corrective instrument.
4: 'al-ta'an kesil ke'evvalto — “do not answer a fool according to his folly.” The first of the famous pair (vv4-5) advises silence: responding on the fool’s own terms (ke'evvalto — according to his folly/in his folly’s mode) transforms the responder into the same kind of person (tishveh-lo gam-'attah — “lest you also become like him”). The warning is about the contaminating effect of engaging on the fool’s terms.
5: 'aneh kesil ke'evvalto — “answer a fool according to his folly.” The immediately following counter-instruction advises response: if you do not answer (pen-yihyeh chakham be'einav — “lest he be wise in his own eyes”), the fool’s false self-assessment goes unchallenged. The two verses together (vv4-5) encode the wisdom of situational judgment: sometimes responding on the fool’s terms ensnares you; sometimes not responding lets him become entrenched in his foolishness. The wisdom tradition preserves both proverbs deliberately, leaving the decision about which situation applies to the practitioner.
6: qotze' raglayim — “cutting off one’s own feet.” The graphic image of self-amputation (qataz, H7112, to cut off; raglayim, feet/legs) applied to the one who sends a message by a fool (sholeach devarim be-yad kesil). The secondary result is shote chamas — “drinking violence” — experiencing the violence that comes back from the foolish messenger’s bungling. The delegator who uses an unfit agent bears the consequences of the agent’s incompetence.
7: dallyu shaqayim me-pisseach — “the legs of the lame hang limp.” The shaqayim (H7785, thighs/legs) of the pisseach (lame person, H6455) that dangle uselessly are compared to mashal be-fi kesilim — “a proverb in the mouth of fools.” The proverb is a tool for the wise; in the mouth of the fool it is as useless as a lame man’s legs: structurally present but functionally inoperative. The wisdom tradition’s self-awareness: its instruments require the right user to work.
8: ki-tzror 'even bimargema — “like tying a stone to a sling.” The margema (H4773, sling-stone heap/sling) with a stone permanently attached (tzror, bound up in) cannot function: the stone can no longer be aimed and released. Applied to ken noten le-khesil kavod — “so is giving honor to a fool”: honor attached to a fool is as counterproductive as a stone tied to its sling. It defeats the purpose of the honor-system rather than serving it.
9: choach 'alah be-yad shikkor — “a thorn-branch in the hand of a drunkard.” The choach (H2336, thorn, brier, specifically a thorny branch) in the hand of a shikkor (H7910, drunk person) is dangerous precisely because the drunkard has no control over where the thorns point. Parallel to mashal be-fi kesilim — a proverb in the mouth of fools: as the drunk brandishes the thorn without direction, the fool brandishes the proverb without discernment, injuring indiscriminately.
10: rav mecholel-kol — “a great archer/employer who wounds all.” The verse is one of the most textually difficult in Proverbs; the MT is uncertain. The MKT follows the reading of a shooter or master-worker (rav, H7227) whose indiscriminate hiring of the fool and the passerby (ve-socher kesil ve-socher 'overim) produces random harm. The managerial wisdom: hiring without discrimination is not generosity but irresponsibility that harms the organization and the employees.
11: ke-kelev shav 'al-qe'o — “like a dog that returns to its vomit.” Kelev (dog, H3611) returning to qe'o (its vomit, H6892) is one of the most vivid images in Proverbs for the fool who repeats his folly. Sakhel shoneh be-'ivvalto — “so is a fool who repeats his foolishness.” The biological compulsion of the animal’s return to vomit is applied to the psychological pattern of the fool who cannot learn from experience. 2 Pet 2:22 quotes this verse exactly, applying it to those who return to a life of sin after knowledge of the truth.
12: ra'ita 'ish chakham be'einav — “do you see a man wise in his own eyes?” The rhetorical question presents the chakham be'einav (wise in his own eyes — the self-assessor who has privileged his own judgment over external reality-testing) as someone in a worse condition than the outright fool. Tiqvah le-khesil mimmenu — “there is more hope for a fool than for him.” The fool knows he is not wise and might be teachable; the self-wise person has closed the feedback loop. This is the wisdom tradition’s most severe critique.
13: 'amar 'atzel shachal badarekh — “the sluggard says: there is a lion in the road.” 'Atzel (H6102, sluggard, lazy person) is the character type who constructs elaborate reasons for inaction. The lion-in-the-road (shachal, H7826, a specific lion term) and the 'ari bein harechavot (lion among the streets) are escalating fantasies of danger. The wisdom tradition’s psychological observation: laziness generates its own rationalizations, and the rationalizations become increasingly implausible as the pattern solidifies.
14: delet tisov 'al-tziyrah — “a door turns on its hinges.” The tziyrah (H6735, hinge/pivot) as the pivot-point around which the door moves without going anywhere is the image of the 'atzel 'al-mittatho — “the sluggard on his bed.” The door swings repeatedly on its hinge: the sluggard turns repeatedly on his bed. Maximum motion, zero progress. The mechanical precision of the image (the door’s kinetic activity that produces no displacement) captures the sluggard’s energetic non-productivity.
15: taman 'atzel yado batzalachat — “the sluggard buries his hand in the dish.” The hand in the bowl (tzalachat, H6745, a dish/bowl) unable to complete the circuit back to the mouth (nil'ah lehashivah el-pihu — “it wearies him to bring it back to his mouth”) is the comic absurdity of laziness taken to its logical extreme. The sluggard has enough motivation to begin eating but not to complete the action. The comedic exaggeration makes the point through humor: laziness, pushed to its extreme, becomes self-destructive in the most basic biological activity.
16: chakham 'atzel be'einav — “the sluggard is wiser in his own eyes.” The sluggard’s self-assessment (chakham be'einav — wise in his own eyes) places him in the category of 26:12: the most dangerous fool is the self-assessed wise person. Applied to shiv'ah meshivim ta'am — “seven men who answer with reason/taste.” The sluggard considers himself wiser than seven thoughtful people; seven being the number of completeness, his self-wisdom exceeds every possible counsel.
17: machaziq be'oznei-khalev 'over — “grabbing the ears of a passing dog.” The image of the mit'abber (H5674 Hithpael, one who passes by, who involves himself) in a quarrel not his own (rib lo' lo — “a quarrel not belonging to him”) is captured by the dog-ear-grabber: the action draws immediate and disproportionate retaliation. Grabbing a dog’s ears transforms a non-threat into an immediate threat. The wisdom observation about the danger of uninvited intervention in others’ conflicts.
18: ke-mitolelah hayoreh ziqim — “like a madman shooting firebrands.” Mitolel (H3856, a madman, one who acts as if deranged) who shoots ziqim (H2131, fire-arrows, firebrands — incendiary missiles) and arrows and death-dealing objects at random: the image is of indiscriminate dangerous behavior by someone with no accountability structure. Applied in v19 to the deceiver who “plays” and then claims it was all a joke.
19: kakhah ha'ish rimmah 'et-re'ehu — “so is the man who deceives his neighbor.” The deceiver who says halo'-mesacheq 'ani — “wasn't I just playing?” — after the harm is done is as dangerous as the firebrand-shooter: the playfulness-claim does not undo the damage. Ramah (H7411, to deceive, to throw down, to beguile) combined with the joke-defense (mesacheq, H7832) represents the use of humor as a shield for malicious action — a form of social weaponization that the wisdom tradition explicitly names and condemns.
20: be-'efes 'etzim tikbeh-'esh — “without wood, a fire goes out.” The elimination of the fuel source as the mechanism of fire-extinction is applied directly to social conflict: uv'ein nirgan yishtoq madon — “and without a gossip/whisperer, strife dies down.” Nirgan (H5372, a whisperer, a murmurer who spreads slanderous reports) is the social fuel of conflict; remove the gossip, and the quarrel has no energy source. The passive yishtoq (becomes quiet, ceases) implies the natural resolution of conflict that has been deprived of its supply line.
21: pechami le-gechalim ve-'etzim le-'esh — “charcoal to embers and wood to fire.” The first parallel of fuel-to-fire adds the specific fuel types: pecham (H6352, charcoal) to gechalim (H1513, glowing embers — cf. Prov 25:22) and wood ('etzim) to fire ('esh). The social parallel is ve-'ish midyanim le-charet madon — “and a contentious man for kindling strife.” Midyan (H4066, contention, strife-causing behavior) and charet (H2750, burning, heating-up) complete the fire-metaphor: the contentious person is the fuel that ignites and sustains social conflict.
22: divrei nirgan ke-mitlahamim — “the words of a gossip are like delicious morsels.” Mitlahamim (H3859, something eagerly swallowed down, delicious morsels — related to the verb meaning to gulp down with pleasure) captures the appealing quality of gossip: it is consumed with relish. Ve-hem yardu chadrei-vaten — “they go down into the inner chambers of the belly.” Chadrei vaten (the innermost chambers of the body) implies deep, lasting penetration: what enters as gossip settles into the deepest recesses of memory and shapes perception in ways that are difficult to dislodge. Gossip is not only appealing but persistent.
23: kesef sigim mezhupach 'al-chares — “silver glaze spread over earthenware.” Sigim (dross/glaze, H5509) — the residue of silver-smelting — spread (mezhupach, H2645, covered with) over chares (earthenware/pottery, H2789) creates the appearance of silver on an object that is actually clay. The appearance of quality covers base material: applied to sefatayim dolqim ve-lev ra' — “fervent/burning lips with an evil heart.” The false surface conceals a contrary interior, which is the definition of the hypocrite in the wisdom tradition.
24: be-sefatav yinnaker sone' — “an enemy disguises himself with his lips.” Nakar (H5234 Niphal, to be recognized — here in the sense of disguising oneself to be unrecognizable) through sefatayim (lips) captures the use of speech as a costume for concealing hatred. Uve-qirbo yasit mirmah — “but he harbors deceit within himself.” Mirmah (H4820, deceit, treachery — the cunning action word for intentional deception) stored in the interior while pleasant words flow externally: the psychology of the hidden enemy.
25: ki-yachnen qolo 'al-ta'amen bo — “when his voice becomes gracious, do not trust him.” Chanan (H2603, to be gracious, to show favor — the root of the name Hannah) applied to an enemy’s voice is the warning sign: exceptional graciousness from someone with a history of enmity is a manipulation signal. Ki sheva' to'avot be-libbo — “for seven abominations are in his heart.” Seven = completeness; the full quota of the detestable is stored behind the pleasant voice.
26: tikkaseh sin'ah be-mirmah — “though hatred covers itself with deceit.” The sequence vv24-26 traces the arc of the hidden enemy: disguise (v24), stored deceit (v24b), gracious voice (v25), and now the moment when the disguise fails: tigaleh ra'ato biqhal — “his wickedness will be exposed in the assembly.” Qahal (H6951, assembly, the gathered community) is the social context in which private concealment becomes public revelation. The wisdom tradition’s confidence that concealed wickedness will be exposed has the assembly as the medium of disclosure.
27: koreh-shachat bah yippol — “whoever digs a pit will fall into it.” The pitfall (shachat, H7845, pit/trap dug for prey) turns against its digger: the mechanism of the trap reverses. Ve-golel 'even 'elav tashuv — “and whoever rolls a stone will have it roll back on them.” The stone-rolling returns to the source by the same gravitational logic. Both images express the principle of the self-defeating trap: malicious action directed against others rebounds on the actor. The Galatians 6:7-8 principle (“whatever one sows, that will he also reap”) is the NT formulation of the same moral physics.
28: lashon sheqer yisna' dakkav — “a lying tongue hates those it crushes.” Dakka' (H1793, crushed/oppressed) as the victim of the lying tongue reveals the hatred beneath the speech act: the lie is not merely a strategic tool but an expression of genuine hatred for the person being destroyed. Ufeh chalaq ya'aseh midcheh — “and a flattering mouth causes ruin.” Midcheh (H4072, overthrow, ruin) as the outcome of peh chalaq (the smooth/flattering mouth): the pleasant speech that is oriented toward destruction is itself an act of hatred.
